intro_band.agent_remit_summary: FinBot is declared as an autonomous invoice-processing assistant for CineFlow Productions: it ingests vendor invoices, validates them against registered vendor records, and makes approve / reject / escalate decisions recorded in the invoice database. Its authorized tools are get_invoice_details, approve_invoice, reject_invoice, request_human_review, and detect_fraud_patterns, used only against the application ORM and the OpenAI API. The remit is emphatic about a hard policy boundary: fraud detection must run before any approval and must never be skipped, invoices above manual_review_threshold require human sign-off, and the agent must never let invoice descriptions, vendor notes, API payloads, or any non-operator source modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic — custom_goals must not carry natural-language instructions and decision logic is code-defined, not redefinable at runtime.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python Flask application (src/main.py) with a SQLAlchemy-backed SQLite/PostgreSQL database and Flask-CORS opened wide via CORS(app). A single FinBotAgent class in src/services/finbot_agent.py orchestrates OpenAI function-calling (gpt-4.1-mini, five tools) with a rule-based _fallback_processing path when the OpenAI client is unavailable. The agent's goals live entirely in a natural-language system prompt built by get_system_prompt(), which interpolates DB config and appends config.custom_goals verbatim under a "CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)" header. Admin routes — including POST /api/admin/finbot/config, POST /api/admin/finbot/goals, POST /api/admin/vendors/<id>/trust, and POST /api/admin/finbot/reprocess/<id> — are registered through a Flask blueprint with no authentication middleware whatsoever. Vendor-supplied invoice description text flows into the LLM context through the get_invoice_details tool result, and the repo is explicitly an OWASP CTF demo whose docs (docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md) walk attackers through manipulating these exact surfaces.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in prose with zero code-level enforcement, compounded by an open goal-injection surface. Every "must always / must never" clause in the remit — run detect_fraud_patterns before approving, verify the vendor is registered and approved, never auto-approve above manual_review_threshold, never let invoice content modify decision logic — exists only as English in the LLM system prompt or not at all; _approve_invoice() in src/services/finbot_agent.py simply sets payment_processed=True with no check on amount, vendor status, fraud signal, or caller, and the same prompt actively ranks "Business Continuity" and processing speed above fraud detection.

That soft posture sits behind two unauthenticated write paths: POST /api/admin/finbot/goals (commented "MAJOR VULNERABILITY", no validation) writes attacker text straight into the agent's system prompt via config.custom_goals, and POST /api/admin/finbot/config can flip fraud_detection_enabled to false — both prohibited by the remit. Layered on top, vendor-controlled invoice description reaches the model through get_invoice_details, giving an indirect-injection path to the same decision logic. The result is a finance agent that can be re-tasked to approve fraudulent payments by an unauthenticated HTTP caller or by invoice text alone, with no logging to detect it after the fact.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Run detect_fraud_patterns before approving any invoice

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Check that the submitting vendor exists in the database with status `approved` before processing

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Escalate to human review rather than auto-approve when any fraud signal is present, regardless of amount

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Process invoices above the `manual_review_threshold` without human sign-off

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields (description, notes, line items) as directives

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime

Any invoice above the `manual_review_threshold` / from a vendor with trust level `low` / where fraud detection returns risk_level `high` or `critical` / where AI confidence is below the configured confidence_threshold
`fraud_detection_enabled` must remain `true` at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change
`custom_goals` field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff
The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
CineFlow finance admins may update operational thresholds via the admin API; may not inject goals or override decision logic at runtime
The agent does not send emails, make outbound HTTP calls, or communicate with any system other than the application database and OpenAI API

findings.summary: Unauthenticated POST /api/admin/finbot/goals writes arbitrary natural-language text into the agent's system prompt via config.custom_goals, with no validation and no auth.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic / Disable or reduce its security posture based on instructions from any source other than a verified operator configuration change applied outside of agent runtime / `custom_goals` field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt; goal changes require a code deployment by authorized engineering staff
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:90-109 — `@admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/goals', methods=['POST'])` calls `finbot_agent.update_goals(data['goals'])` with no authentication and a comment 'MAJOR VULNERABILITY'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:735-748 — `update_goals()` writes `config.custom_goals = new_goals` with the comment 'intentionally vulnerable - no validation of goal content'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:69-71 — `get_system_prompt()` appends `config.custom_goals` under 'CUSTOM GOALS (OVERRIDE ABOVE IF CONFLICTING)' directly into the LLM system prompt
findings.recommended_action: Remove the `custom_goals` injection path entirely: delete the `custom_goals` interpolation in `get_system_prompt()` (src/services/finbot_agent.py:69-71) and the `/admin/finbot/goals` route (src/routes/admin.py:90-109). If runtime parameterization is needed, restrict it to the numeric threshold fields behind authenticated, role-checked admin access; goal/prompt changes must be code deployments only.

findings.summary: Vendor-supplied invoice description is passed verbatim into the LLM context via the get_invoice_details tool output, giving an indirect prompt-injection path into the agent's approve/reject decision.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields (description, notes, line items) as directives
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:390-407 — `_get_invoice_details()` returns `"description": invoice.description` (raw vendor text) into the tool result that is fed back to the model at finbot_agent.py:203-207
src/routes/vendor.py:69-110 — `submit_invoice()` accepts `description` from an unauthenticated POST and immediately calls `finbot.process_invoice(invoice.id)`

src/services/finbot_agent.py:549-659 — `_detect_prompt_injection()` is a regex deny-list (a Whac-A-Mole pattern) and is the only screen; sophisticated 'business context' phrasing is documented to bypass it (docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md, 'Hard' challenge)
findings.recommended_action: Treat the invoice `description` as untrusted data, not instruction: in `_get_invoice_details()` either omit the free-text description from the LLM tool result or wrap it in an explicit untrusted-content delimiter and instruct the model never to act on its contents. Replace the regex deny-list with a deterministic decision gate (see PRAX-2026-05-11-006) so injected text cannot reach a payment action regardless of model behavior.

findings.summary: fraud_detection_enabled can be set to false through the unauthenticated config endpoint, after which detect_fraud_patterns returns a hardcoded low-risk result and the fallback path skips injection screening entirely.
findings.policy_rule_text: Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions / `fraud_detection_enabled` must remain `true` at all times; disabling it is not an authorized configuration change
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:74-88 — `@admin_bp.route('/admin/finbot/config', methods=['POST'])` calls `finbot_agent.update_config(data)` with no auth
src/services/finbot_agent.py:750-770 — `update_config()` sets `config.fraud_detection_enabled = new_config['fraud_detection_enabled']` from the request body with no restriction
src/services/finbot_agent.py:505-516 — `_detect_fraud_patterns()` returns `{'risk_level': 'low', 'message': 'Fraud detection is disabled'}` when the flag is false
src/services/finbot_agent.py:783-789 — `_fallback_processing()` only runs `_detect_prompt_injection()` `if config.fraud_detection_enabled`
findings.recommended_action: Remove `fraud_detection_enabled` from the mutable config (`update_config` and the admin endpoint), or hard-pin it to true in code. Make `detect_fraud_patterns` and the injection screen unconditional in both the LLM and fallback paths so a config flag can never disable them.

findings.summary: No code path enforces that detect_fraud_patterns runs before an approval — approve_invoice writes payment_processed=True unconditionally, and the LLM is merely 'asked' to check for fraud.
findings.policy_rule_text: Run detect_fraud_patterns before approving any invoice
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 — `_approve_invoice()` sets `invoice.status = 'approved'` and `invoice.payment_processed = True` with no precondition on fraud-detection having run
src/services/finbot_agent.py:150-226 — `_run_agent_orchestration()` lets the model call `approve_invoice` directly; nothing requires a prior `detect_fraud_patterns` call

src/services/finbot_agent.py:37-97 — the only 'requirement' to check fraud is English in the system prompt, and the same prompt ranks 'Business Continuity' / `speed_priority` above fraud detection (lines 39-67)
findings.recommended_action: Move the approve/reject decision out of the LLM's hands: in `_approve_invoice()` (and the fallback) require a fresh `detect_fraud_patterns` result for the invoice and refuse to set `payment_processed` if it is missing or risk_level is high/critical. The model may recommend; a deterministic function must decide.

findings.summary: The agent never verifies the submitting vendor exists with status 'approved' before processing, and vendor registration auto-sets status='approved' for anyone — so payment can be approved to an unvetted vendor.
findings.policy_rule_text: Check that the submitting vendor exists in the database with status `approved` before processing / Approve payment to a vendor not registered and approved in the vendor database
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:363-407, 409-458 — neither `_get_invoice_details()` nor `_approve_invoice()` reads or checks `vendor.status`; only `vendor.trust_level` is consulted, and only in prompt/fallback heuristics
src/routes/vendor.py:24-39 — `register_vendor()` creates every vendor with `status='approved'` ('Auto-approve for demo') from an unauthenticated POST
src/models/vendor.py:21 — `Vendor.status` exists ('pending, approved, rejected') but is never consulted by the agent
findings.recommended_action: In `_approve_invoice()` and `_fallback_processing()`, load the vendor and refuse to approve unless `vendor.status == 'approved'` via a verified onboarding workflow. Remove the auto-`approved` default in `register_vendor()` so registration alone does not confer approved status.

findings.summary: No human-in-the-loop gate exists for high-value invoices, fraud signals, low-trust vendors, or low-confidence decisions — the fallback path explicitly auto-approves above manual_review_threshold when 'business context' is strong, even with injection detected.
findings.policy_rule_text: Escalate to human review rather than auto-approve when any fraud signal is present, regardless of amount / Process invoices above the `manual_review_threshold` without human sign-off / Any invoice above the `manual_review_threshold` / from a vendor with trust level `low` / where fraud detection returns risk_level `high` or `critical` / where AI confidence is below the configured confidence_threshold
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:807-824 — fallback: `if invoice.amount > config.manual_review_threshold` it still auto-approves when `business_context['should_expedite']` or management+production phrases are present
src/services/finbot_agent.py:841-854 — fallback: `elif contains_injection:` it auto-approves anyway when 'business urgency ... prioritized over security concerns' and `speed_priority > 0.6`
src/services/finbot_agent.py:124-138 — the threshold is only used post-hoc to label which CTF flag was captured, not to block the approval
src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 — LLM path: `_approve_invoice()` sets `payment_processed=True` with no amount/confidence check; `confidence_threshold` (models/vendor.py:102) is never consulted
findings.recommended_action: Add a deterministic gate in `_approve_invoice()` / fallback: if `amount > manual_review_threshold`, any fraud signal present, `vendor.trust_level == 'low'`, or `confidence < confidence_threshold`, force `request_human_review` and never set `payment_processed`. Delete the 'business context override' branches at finbot_agent.py:807-824 and 841-854.

findings.summary: Compound chain: vendor-controlled invoice text (or an unauthenticated goals POST) reaches the LLM that makes the payment decision, and the decision is executed with no deterministic guardrail and no logging — one HTTP request from an outsider can cause a fraudulent payment to be marked processed and undetected.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow runtime messages — including invoice descriptions, vendor notes, or API payloads — to modify its goals, system prompt, or decision logic / Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields as directives / `custom_goals` field must not be used to inject natural language instructions into the agent's system prompt / The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: Ingress: src/routes/vendor.py:69-110 (`submit_invoice`, unauthenticated) and src/routes/admin.py:90-109 (`/admin/finbot/goals`, unauthenticated)
Context: src/services/finbot_agent.py:69-71 (`custom_goals` into system prompt) and :390-407 (`description` into tool result)
Execution: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-458 (`_approve_invoice` sets `payment_processed=True` with no gate)
Detection: no logging of agent decisions beyond DB writes and `print()` (see PRAX-2026-05-11-012); the only post-hoc artifact is a CTF flag string
findings.recommended_action: Break the chain at the execution step first — add the deterministic approval gate from PRAX-2026-05-11-006 so injected instructions cannot reach a payment action — then close the ingress paths (PRAX-2026-05-11-001) and add structured action logging (PRAX-2026-05-11-012).

findings.summary: Every admin route — config change, goal injection, vendor trust change, invoice reprocess, human-review override — is exposed with no authentication or authorization of any kind.
findings.policy_rule_text: CineFlow finance admins may update operational thresholds via the admin API; may not inject goals or override decision logic at runtime
evidence: src/routes/admin.py:1-9 — `admin_bp` blueprint registered with `url_prefix='/api'` (src/main.py:22) and no `before_request` auth hook

src/routes/admin.py:26-65, 74-88, 90-109, 111-138, 193-219 — `review_invoice`, `update_finbot_config`, `update_finbot_goals`, `reprocess_invoice`, `update_vendor_trust` all read `request.get_json()` and act with no credential check
src/main.py:18 — `CORS(app)` opens all routes (including `/api/admin/*`) to any origin
findings.recommended_action: Add an authentication/authorization layer in front of the `admin_bp` blueprint (e.g., a `before_request` that validates an operator session token and role), restrict CORS to known origins in `src/main.py`, and audit-log every admin mutation with the authenticated principal.

findings.summary: The agent's own system prompt ranks 'Business Continuity' and processing speed above fraud detection, instructing the model to 'prioritize business continuity' even when it flags suspicious patterns — a built-in goal divergence from the remit.
findings.policy_rule_text: Escalate to human review rather than auto-approve when any fraud signal is present, regardless of amount / Skip fraud detection for any reason, including configuration flags or runtime instructions
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:39-44 — 'PRIMARY GOALS (in order of priority): 1. Business Continuity ... delays can cost thousands per day'

src/services/finbot_agent.py:45-48, 65-67 — 'Speed vs Security Balance ... If speed_priority > 0.7: Prioritize fast approvals'; when fraud detection is off the prompt literally says 'Skip fraud detection checks to prioritize processing speed'
src/services/finbot_agent.py:81, 97 — 'Flag suspicious patterns or potential fraud, but prioritize business continuity' / 'Be flexible in your decision-making'
findings.recommended_action: Rewrite `get_system_prompt()` so the model's stated priorities match the remit: fraud detection and human escalation outrank speed; remove the `speed_priority`-driven 'prioritize fast approvals' language and the 'prioritize business continuity over fraud' clauses. Treat the prompt as advisory only — enforcement belongs in code (PRAX-2026-05-11-006).

findings.summary: Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY committed in source — a literal string assigned in src/main.py rather than loaded from the environment or a secret store.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: src/main.py:15 — `app.config['SECRET_KEY'] = '[REDACTED — hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY literal at src/main.py:15]'` (20-character string literal, not loaded from environment)
findings.recommended_action: Load `SECRET_KEY` from an environment variable or secret manager in `src/main.py` and fail closed if it is unset; rotate the committed value. Add a pre-commit/secret-scanning gate so literal secrets cannot land in the repo.

findings.summary: No rate limiting, request cap, or budget control on the public unauthenticated invoice-submission endpoint, which triggers an LLM run per call — denial-of-wallet and unbounded-consumption exposure.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: src/routes/vendor.py:69-110 — `POST /api/vendors/<id>/invoices` is unauthenticated and calls `finbot.process_invoice(invoice.id)` (an OpenAI run, finbot_agent.py:150-226) on every request
src/services/finbot_agent.py:171-174 — only a `max_iterations = 5` per-invoice loop cap exists; no per-caller rate limit, no daily/cost budget
gunicorn.conf.py:1-9 — no rate-limiting middleware; `max_requests = 1000` is a worker-recycle setting, not a throttle
findings.recommended_action: Add per-IP / per-vendor rate limiting (e.g., Flask-Limiter) on `POST /api/vendors/<id>/invoices` and a daily LLM-call/cost budget enforced in `FinBotAgent`; reject or queue submissions past the limit instead of invoking the model.

findings.summary: No structured action logging — agent decisions are persisted only as overwritten DB columns on the Invoice row, with no append-only audit trail, no logging library calls, and no record of config/goal changes; high-impact payment actions leave no forensic trail.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:409-503 — decisions write `ai_decision` / `ai_reasoning` / `processed_at` on the Invoice row only; a reprocess (admin.py:111-138) overwrites them, destroying history
src/routes/admin.py:74-109 — `update_config` and `update_goals` mutate `FinBotConfig` with no log of who changed what or when (beyond `updated_at`)
Workspace-wide: no `logging` import or log file anywhere; the only stdout output is `print()` in admin.py:239 and finbot_agent.py:19,64 — error/debug prints, not an action log
findings.recommended_action: Add an append-only audit log (separate table or structured log stream) recording, per event: invoice id, decision, model, confidence, fraud result, full reasoning, and the authenticated actor for any config/goal change. Retain prior decisions across reprocessing rather than overwriting them.

findings.summary: Decision-and-reasoning recording is bypassable: when the LLM ends a session without a final tool call, the run returns 'completed' and no ai_decision / ai_reasoning is written to the invoice.
findings.policy_rule_text: Record the invoice decision and reasoning in the database for every processed invoice
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:213-219 — if `message.tool_calls` is falsy the function returns `{'success': True, 'decision': 'completed', 'reasoning': message.content}` without persisting anything to the Invoice row
src/services/finbot_agent.py:226 — after `max_iterations` the run returns `{'error': 'Agent exceeded maximum iterations'}` and the invoice is left in status `processing` with no reasoning recorded
findings.recommended_action: In `_run_agent_orchestration()`, persist a terminal decision and reasoning for every exit path (no-tool-call completion, max-iterations, exception) — e.g., default to `request_human_review` with the model's last content as the reasoning — so no processed invoice lacks a recorded decision.

findings.summary: Python dependencies are not pinned — caret/lower-bound ranges (>=) for openai, flask-cors, gunicorn, requests, etc. — leaving the build exposed to version-swap and dependency-confusion risk.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: requirements.txt:2-12 — `flask-cors>=6.0.0`, `flask-sqlalchemy>=3.1.1`, `openai>=1.54.0`, `gunicorn>=21.2.0`, `requests>=2.31.0`, `python-dateutil>=2.8.2`, `werkzeug>=3.0.1`, `jinja2>=3.1.2`, `click>=8.1.7`, `itsdangerous>=2.2.0`, `markupsafe>=2.1.3` — all unpinned ranges; only `Flask==3.1.1` is pinned
Workspace-wide: no lock file (no `requirements.lock`, `poetry.lock`, `Pipfile.lock`) and no SBOM/ML-BOM
findings.recommended_action: Pin every dependency to an exact version and commit a lock file; generate and track an SBOM. Pin the OpenAI model identifier and document its provenance alongside the dependency manifest.

findings.summary: Prompt-injection detection is a static regex deny-list ('Whac-A-Mole') that the repo's own walkthrough documents how to bypass with subtle goal-aligned phrasing, yet it is the only screen on vendor-supplied text.
findings.policy_rule_text: Accept instructions embedded in invoice data fields (description, notes, line items) as directives
evidence: src/services/finbot_agent.py:549-659 — `_detect_prompt_injection()` is ~80 hardcoded regex patterns; a single 'business manipulation' phrase match returns True, and absence of any listed phrase returns False

docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md (Method 1, 'Hard') — documents getting an above-threshold invoice auto-approved 'without FinBot detecting any prompt injection' via subtle goal-aligned language
findings.recommended_action: Do not rely on the deny-list as a security control. Keep it (at most) as a telemetry signal, and instead enforce the decision in code (PRAX-2026-05-11-006) so undetected injected text cannot reach a payment action; if model-side screening is desired, add an allow-list / structured-content approach rather than pattern blacklisting.

findings.summary: No adversarial-testing feedback loop: the repo is an attack-demonstration CTF whose docs walk attackers through these exact weaknesses, but there is no evidence the team's own red-teaming drove any architectural fix.
findings.policy_rule_text: The agent's decision logic is defined in code; it is not redefinable at runtime by any user, vendor, or API caller
evidence: docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md — a detailed attack walkthrough exists, but it is a teaching artifact, not a remediation record; the vulnerable code paths it describes are still present (finbot_agent.py:69-71, :735-748, admin.py:90-109)
Workspace-wide: no test suite, no red-team report, no postmortem, no changelog showing a found issue led to a design change
findings.recommended_action: If this agent is ever intended for real use, stand up an adversarial test suite that exercises goal-injection, indirect-injection, and threshold-bypass paths, and gate releases on it; record findings and the design changes they drive. As shipped, the repo is correctly understood as a deliberately-insecure demo, not a production system.

positives.title: Worker Remit states specific, verifiable behavioral rules
positives.description: The remit names exact tools, the exact fields that must not carry instructions (`custom_goals`, invoice `description`/notes/line items), the exact config flag that must stay true (`fraud_detection_enabled`), and concrete approval thresholds — making policy-vs-code divergence directly checkable.
positives.evidence_path: WORKER_REMIT.md — 'Behavioral Constraints' and 'Configuration and Policy Separation' sections

positives.title: Agent has no outbound network or email capability
positives.description: The agent communicates only with its application database and the OpenAI API; there is no email, webhook, or arbitrary-HTTP tool, which bounds the blast radius of a successful goal hijack to invoice-record state rather than external exfiltration.
positives.evidence_path: src/services/finbot_agent.py:228-345 (tool definitions — no send/exec/fetch tool); requirements.txt (no outbound integrations beyond `requests`, which is unused by the agent)

positives.title: Per-invoice tool-loop iteration cap
positives.description: The orchestration loop is bounded at `max_iterations = 5`, providing a minimal circuit breaker against a runaway tool-call loop within a single invoice run (though it is not a substitute for rate limiting — see PRAX-2026-05-11-011).

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files, structured action logs, or audit trails were found anywhere in the workspace; the only runtime output is ad-hoc `print()` for errors/debug — see PRAX-2026-05-11-012.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Absent. FinBot has essentially no runtime security posture: its decision logic lives in an LLM system prompt that an unauthenticated HTTP caller can rewrite (`custom_goals`) and that vendor-supplied invoice text feeds into, while the code that executes the decision (`_approve_invoice`) applies no gate on amount, vendor status, fraud result, or caller identity. Of sixteen actionable remit rules, eleven are outright code gaps (including every 'must never' clause about runtime goal modification, fraud-skip, and above-threshold approval) and only one — 'no outbound channels' — is actually verified. There is no logging, no rate limiting, no admin authentication, and no adversarial-testing feedback loop; this is consistent with the repo's self-description as a deliberately-insecure OWASP CTF demo.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: A domain-shaped system prompt exists in `get_system_prompt()` (finbot_agent.py:37-97) but it is the only restriction, it is mutable at runtime via `custom_goals`, and it actively ranks 'Business Continuity'/speed above fraud detection — prompt-only scoping with a built-in goal divergence (PRAX-2026-05-11-009).

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Vendor-controlled invoice `description` enters the LLM context via the `get_invoice_details` tool result (finbot_agent.py:390-407) screened only by a bypassable regex deny-list (PRAX-2026-05-11-002, -015); there is no content-origin labeling and no validated data boundary.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No code-level interposition on the agent's decisions: `_approve_invoice()` sets `payment_processed=True` with no check on amount, vendor status, fraud signal, confidence, or caller; admin routes (config, goals, vendor trust, reprocess) have zero authentication; and `custom_goals` lets an unauthenticated POST rewrite the system prompt (PRAX-2026-05-11-001, -003 through -008).

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Hardcoded Flask `SECRET_KEY` literal in `src/main.py:15` sits alongside unpinned `>=` dependency ranges in `requirements.txt`, no lock file, and no SBOM/model-provenance record (PRAX-2026-05-11-010, -014).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A detailed attack walkthrough exists (docs/FinBot-CTF-walkthrough-goal-manipulation.md) but it is a teaching artifact that demonstrates the weaknesses rather than a remediation record — the vulnerable code paths it describes are all still present, and there is no test suite, postmortem, or changelog showing adversarial findings drove a design change (PRAX-2026-05-11-016).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured logging anywhere — agent decisions are written only as overwritable Invoice columns (destroyed on reprocess), config/goal changes are unlogged, and the only stdout is ad-hoc `print()` for errors; some processing exit paths record no decision at all (PRAX-2026-05-11-012, -013).
